message:
feat: tool-aware MCP config rewriting, recipients_json denormalization, SQLite lock retry, snapshot hardening, and search/guard improvements

CLI (lib.rs +473, robot.rs, e2e_runner.rs):
- Add retry_sync_sqlite_lock wrapper around all synchronous SQLite open/init/health
  paths so transient SQLITE_BUSY errors are retried with back-off instead of failing
- Rewrite MCP config doctor-fix to be tool-aware: each JSON config target (Claude,
  Cursor, Gemini, OpenCode, Codex, etc.) now emits the correct transport key (url vs
  httpUrl), type field (http vs remote), and Authorization headers instead of a
  one-size-fits-all url-only rewrite
- Migrate TOML config from bearer_token_env_var to http_headers inline table so auth
  tokens flow through TOML configs the same way as JSON ones
- Fix inbox priority_bucket ordering: urgent/high-unread is now bucket 1 (was 2),
  ack-overdue is bucket 2 (was 1), collapsing 7 buckets to 6 for cleaner semantics
- Eliminate N+1 outbox/thread queries by folding recipient_count and acked_count into
  the main GROUP BY query instead of per-message sub-selects
- Fix e2e_runner pipe deadlock: stdout/stderr are now drained in background threads
  before wait() to prevent child blocking on full pipe buffers
- Add tests for transient-lock retry on both open and health-check paths
- Add tests for Gemini httpUrl and OpenCode remote-type MCP config rewriting

DB layer (pool.rs +117, queries.rs, models.rs, schema.rs, migrate.rs, search_*):
- Add recipients_json TEXT column to messages table (migration v15) to store
  denormalized to/cc/bcc recipient name lists, avoiding per-message agent lookups
- Populate recipients_json at message creation time inside the concurrent transaction
- Thread recipients_json through all inbox, thread, global inbox, unacked, and search
  queries (column index shifts in positional row.get_as calls)
- Add SQLite identity-path cache (Mutex<HashMap>) with 2s freshness window and 256-entry
  cap to avoid repeated fs::canonicalize syscalls on hot pool-acquisition paths
- Decommission FTS5-backed search_messages/search_messages_for_product/global in favor
  of LIKE-term fallback (br-2tnl.8.4) — removes fragile FTS MATCH queries that broke
  on schema drift
- Fix ACTIVE_RESERVATION_PREDICATE to use fully-qualified file_reservations.released_ts
  columns so the predicate works correctly when the table is aliased (e.g. "fr")
- Fix get_active_reservations and list_file_reservations to use LEFT JOIN on
  file_reservation_releases with the alias-safe predicate
- Fix inbox_stats trigger to use MAX(COALESCE(...)) for last_message_ts to prevent
  out-of-order message timestamps from regressing the stat
- Fix extract_like_terms to deduplicate case-insensitively
- Raise search limit ceiling from 1000 to 100_000 to support deep pagination offsets
- Fix migrate.rs strip_timezone_suffix to check is_char_boundary before slicing
- Add SearchResult::default() impl with new to/cc/bcc fields; update all call sites
- Add candidate budget re-allocation when one channel (lexical or semantic) is bypassed
- search_service: populate to/cc/bcc on canonicalized message results

Guard (guard/lib.rs):
- Pre-compute normalized_pattern and has_glob on FileReservationRecord at load time
  instead of re-normalizing on every conflict check iteration
- Fix Python hook normalize_match_input to collapse redundant path segments (./ ../)
  and normalize slashes, matching Rust-side normalization
- Fix Python hook glob_match to guard against empty normalized paths/patterns
- Remove symmetric glob_match (pattern-as-path) which caused false-positive conflicts;
  keep only concrete-path-matches-glob direction
- Fix is_expired to treat unparseable timestamps as expired (fail-open → fail-closed)
- Promote contains_glob from test-only to module-level function
- Fix get_push_paths test to use detached HEAD workflow for reliable push-path detection

Share (scope.rs, snapshot.rs +646):
- Refactor snapshot KNOWN_TABLES from tuple array to KnownTable struct with explicit
  page_by_column, primary_key_columns, and columns fields
- Add file_reservation_releases, inbox_stats, and tool_metrics_snapshots to snapshot
  table list
- Add recipients_json column to messages snapshot definition
- Replace hardcoded "SELECT id FROM projects" source probe with sqlite_master catalog
  query so snapshots work on databases without the projects table yet
- Add source_columns_frank/source_columns_c introspection to discover which columns
  actually exist in the source, enabling graceful column-mismatch handling
- Add snapshot_default_value for missing columns (e.g. recipients_json → "{}")
- Generalize pagination from "id"-only to any page_by_column with extract_page_value
- scope.rs: clean up file_reservation_releases orphans when filtering out reservations

Tools (messaging.rs, products.rs, reservations.rs, resources.rs, search.rs):
- Surface to/cc/bcc recipient lists on InboxMessage, MessageDetails, ThreadMessageEntry,
  and SearchResult by parsing recipients_json; bcc is only shown to the sender
- Add ranking parameter to search_messages_product for explicit ranking mode override
- Fix reservation renewal and release to resolve glob patterns against active
  reservations in the tool layer before passing resolved IDs to the DB layer, so that
  "release src/**" correctly matches "src/main.rs" reservations
- Deduplicate summary key_points and action_items in summarize_messages
- Fix is_ordered_prefix to accept multi-digit list prefixes (e.g. "10. item")
- Remove unused backtick_snippets function
- Fix search to pass next_cursor through regardless of offset value
- Remove redundant planner_limit cap at 10_000 now that limit ceiling is 100_000

Server (lib.rs, tui_screens/):
- Thread Cx (cancellation context) through HTTP auth pipeline: check_bearer_auth,
  decode_jwt, fetch_jwks, jwt_decoding_key, and check_rbac_and_rate_limit now accept
  &Cx for proper request-scoped deadline propagation
- Add request_cx() helper that derives a Budget from request_timeout_secs
- Fix STARTUP_SEARCH_BACKFILL_IN_PROGRESS reset to use SeqCst ordering
- Fix dashboard ratio_bar minimum-fill logic
- Add char_boundary guard to TUI reservation custom TTL parsing

Core (mcp_config.rs, setup.rs, toon.rs):
- mcp_config: strip HTTP transport fields (url, httpUrl, headers, type, enabled) when
  build_updated_server_entry switches an entry back to command/stdio mode, preventing
  stale remote-mode keys from confusing MCP clients
- setup.rs: generate http_headers inline TOML table instead of bearer_token_env_var
- toon.rs: fix parse_saved_line percentage extraction when '(' appears before '%' at
  a different position than expected

Agent detection (agent-detect/lib.rs):
- Deduplicate KNOWN_CONNECTORS slugs before detection to prevent duplicate entries when
  multiple connector aliases resolve to the same canonical slug

Infrastructure (install.sh +389, scripts/am, README.md, MCP configs, fixtures):
- install.sh: rewrite setup_single_toml_config from append-only to full AWK-based
  in-place TOML rewriter that sets url + http_headers and strips stale command/args
- install.sh: add setup_single_codex_json_config with Python-based JSON rewriter for
  Codex CLI configs, handling BOM, comments, trailing commas
- install.sh: add normalize_mcp_http_path, desired_mcp_http_url, and
  resolve_setup_http_bearer_token helpers
- install.sh: move HTTP_BEARER_TOKEN resolution before server-reuse check so the token
  is available for MCP config sync during reuse
- scripts/am: migrate sync_toml_mcp_url from bearer_token_env_var to http_headers;
  move bearer token resolution before reuse check for same reason as install.sh
- Update codex.mcp.json, cursor.mcp.json, gemini.mcp.json URL path from /mcp/ to /api/
- Update doctor_check.json test fixture for updated check output
- Update README.md with revised setup instructions

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"br-154k","title":"T2.3: Implement multi-seed flake detection (N re-runs with different seeds, parallel)","description":"## Objective
Add deterministic multi-seed flake detection to distinguish nondeterministic failures from stable failures.

## Work
- Implement rerun orchestration across configurable seed sets and run counts.
- Support safe parallel execution while preserving deterministic result aggregation.
- Classify outcomes into stable-fail, flaky, and inconclusive buckets with clear evidence.

## Deliverable
A native flake detector that provides reproducible, high-confidence flakiness signals.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation is native Rust and cross-platform, introducing no new operational dependency on external shell/Python tooling.
- Preserves required legacy behavior (or documented intentional changes) with deterministic CLI/API semantics and stable `--json` output where applicable.
- Emits structured diagnostics and runtime telemetry sufficient for user-facing troubleshooting and CI artifact capture.
- Has comprehensive test coverage tied to this behavior (unit + integration + e2e where relevant), including negative-path assertions.
- Demonstrates reproducible behavior under CI constraints (timeouts, retries, deterministic ordering/seed controls).
- Produces explicit logging output suitable for rapid root-cause analysis when failures occur.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:24:32.145033710Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T04:48:50.536523869Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T04:48:50.536502700Z","close_reason":"Implemented by RubyPrairie: extend_seeds(), run_multi_seed_subprocess(), MultiSeedConfig with 5 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-154k","depends_on_id":"br-1z66","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":65,"issue_id":"br-154k","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# T2.3: Implement Multi-Seed Flake Detection

## What to build
Run a specific test N times with different seeds to determine if it's a true flake
(intermittent failure) vs a hard failure (always fails). Replaces the detect mode
in flake_triage.sh.

## Current bash behavior (lines ~100-140 of flake_triage.sh)
```bash
for seed in $(seq 1 $num_seeds); do
    if cargo test "$test_name" -- --seed "$seed" 2>/dev/null; then
        passes=$((passes + 1))
    else
        fails=$((fails + 1))
    fi
done
```

## Rust implementation
```rust
struct FlakeDetectionConfig {
    test_name: String,
    num_seeds: u32,        // default: 10
    parallel: bool,        // run seeds concurrently
    timeout_per_run: Duration,  // default: 60s
    cargo_args: Vec<String>,    // extra args to pass to cargo test
}

struct FlakeDetectionResult {
    test_name: String,
    total_runs: u32,
    passes: u32,
    failures: u32,
    is_flaky: bool,         // true if 0 < failures < total_runs
    flake_rate: f64,        // failures / total_runs
    failing_seeds: Vec<u64>,
    passing_seeds: Vec<u64>,
    elapsed: Duration,
}

fn detect_flake(config: &FlakeDetectionConfig) -> Result<FlakeDetectionResult, Error>
```

## Implementation notes
- Use std::process::Command to run `cargo test <test_name>` with `--seed <N>` if supported,
  or with env var `TEST_SEED=<N>` as an alternative
- For --parallel mode, use std::thread::scope to run seeds concurrently
  (limit concurrency to num_cpus to avoid oversubscription)
- Capture exit code only (not full output) for speed
- Timeout: kill the subprocess if it exceeds timeout_per_run

## Location

The exact cargo test invocation in multi-seed mode targets THREE packages:
  HARNESS_SEED="$seed" cargo test -p mcp-agent-mail-core -p mcp-agent-mail-server -p mcp-agent-mail-db "$test_name" -- --nocapture >/dev/null 2>&1

The DEFAULT_FLAKE_SEEDS (17 values) are ALREADY defined in core::flake_triage (lines 346-368):
0, 1, 2, 42, 100, 255, 1000, 12345, 65535, 999_999, 0xDEAD_BEEF, 0xCAFE_BABE, 0x1234_5678, 0xFFFF_FFFF, u64::MAX, u64::MAX/2, u64::MAX/3

When N > 17, the bash extends with $RANDOM$RANDOM. Rust should extend with thread_rng().

The run_with_seeds() function already exists in core::flake_triage. The CLI just needs to:
1. Create a closure that spawns `cargo test` with HARNESS_SEED env var
2. Pass it to run_with_seeds()
3. Format the resulting FlakeReport

Per-seed output should be colorized (like bash): green PASS / red FAIL with seed value.

 {"id":"br-15dv","title":"[epic] Extreme Performance & Resilience: 1000+ Concurrent Agents","description":"## Goal
Make **MCP Agent Mail (Rust)** operationally boring at extreme concurrency: **1000+ agents** performing reads+writes simultaneously without deadlocks, data loss, unbounded memory growth, or IO collapse.

This project is already a feature-parity Rust port of the legacy Python server. This epic is explicitly about going beyond parity: hard performance budgets, reliability engineering, and overload-safe architecture.

## Why This Exists (Failure Modes From Python Era)
The original Python implementation degraded badly under high concurrency due to:
- SQLite contention and SQLITE_BUSY storms during bursts.
- Git index.lock contention and expensive synchronous git operations on hot paths.
- Unbounded in-memory queues and log/event amplification under load.
- Cascading IO failures (many tiny writes, lock thrash, background tasks competing with the hot path).

## Non-Negotiables
- Correctness: No lost messages/acks/reservations/contacts. DB remains the source of truth.
- Bounded resources: Every queue has a capacity and explicit policy when full.
- Graceful degradation: Under overload, shed non-critical work first and return actionable errors (429/503 with retry guidance); never cliff-drop into slow synchronous IO.
- Deterministic validation: Every optimization must preserve conformance fixtures/golden outputs (isomorphism proof) unless we intentionally change the contract and update fixtures.

## Target Load Model (Working Assumption)
- Agents: 1000 registered agents across 1-50 projects.
- Concurrency: 100-500 concurrent HTTP requests (thundering herd patterns included).
- Mix: read-heavy (fetch_inbox/resources/search) with periodic write bursts (send_message, reservations, acks).
- Pathologies: message storms (1000 msgs in <1s), inbox polling storms, many reservations, large payload attempts, worker failure, disk pressure.

## Strategy (Extreme Optimization Loop)
1. Baseline representative scenarios (p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput, RSS growth, queue depths).
2. Instrument the true bottlenecks (DB pool waits, SQLite busy, WBQ/commit backlog, lock contention).
3. Change one lever at a time (Opportunity Matrix score >= 2.0), with a rollback plan.
4. Prove behavior unchanged (conformance + golden outputs + invariants).
5. Repeat: bottlenecks shift.

## Consistency Model (Intentional)
- SQLite DB is authoritative for queryable state (messages, recipients, reservations, contacts, acks).
- Git archive is an async audit log, best-effort and overload-safe. It must never block tool responses.
- Signals/notifications are correctness-adjacent (UX responsiveness) and must be explicitly categorized as required vs skippable under overload.

## Tracks
Work is organized into tracks under this epic: db, storage/git, concurrency/backpressure, memory, algorithms, resilience, build+bench, observability, stress/chaos.","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance Criteria:

- All queues are bounded; overflow policies are explicit and tested.

- cargo test, cargo clippy -D warnings, cargo fmt --check pass.
- Crash/restart test: kill server mid-burst, restart, PRAGMA integrity_check passes and system resumes without manual intervention.

Epic complete.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["perf","resilience","scale"],"comments":[{"id":67,"issue_id":"br-15dv","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ARCHITECTURE CONTEXT: The Python mcp_agent_mail used SQLAlchemy (async) + GitPython with no connection pooling, no write batching, and blocking git operations on every message send. It regularly failed at 10+ concurrent agents due to: (1) SQLite 'database is locked' errors from too many writers, (2) git index.lock storms when multiple agents committed simultaneously, (3) memory leaks from unclosed DB sessions, (4) cascading timeouts when one slow git operation blocked the entire system. CURRENT CODE (pool.rs:24-27):
  DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE = 3
  DEFAULT_MAX_OVERFLOW = 4
  DEFAULT_POOL_TIMEOUT_MS = 60_000

SOLUTION: Implement adaptive pool sizing that scales with observed load:
1. Increase defaults to min=20, max=100 (SQLite WAL handles concurrent readers well)
2. Add adaptive sizing: monitor acquire latency, grow pool when p95 > 50ms, shrink when utilization < 30%
3. Separate read pool (large, many connections) from write pool (small, serialized via WAL)
4. Add pool warmup on startup (pre-open min connections)
5. Add per-pool metrics: active, idle, waiting, acquire_latency_p95

VALIDATION: stress_pool_exhaustion_recovery test must pass with pool_size=20, 200 concurrent threads.

 {"id":"br-3i7fz","title":"R1.3: Implement format dispatcher — serialize any Serialize value to chosen OutputFormat","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:16:48.220987782Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T04:43:30.506024687Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T04:43:30.506006173Z","close_reason":"format_output() and format_output_md() dispatchers already implemented in robot.rs — JSON/TOON/Markdown with MarkdownRenderable trait","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3i7fz","depends_on_id":"br-2gg5q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:05Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":812,"issue_id":"br-3i7fz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R1.3: Format Dispatcher

## What
A single function that takes a `RobotEnvelope<T>` and an `OutputFormat`, and produces the final output string. This is the central serialization gateway for ALL robot commands.

## Where
- Same module as OutputFormat/RobotEnvelope (robot.rs or lib.rs)

## Implementation
```rust
pub fn format_output<T: Serialize>(envelope: &RobotEnvelope<T>, format: OutputFormat) -> Result<String, CliError> {
    match format {
        OutputFormat::Json => {
            serde_json::to_string_pretty(envelope).map_err(|e| CliError::Format(e.to_string()))
        }
        OutputFormat::Toon => {
            let json_str = serde_json::to_string(envelope)?;
            toon::json_to_toon(&json_str).map_err(|e| CliError::Format(e.to_string()))
        }
        OutputFormat::Markdown => {
            // Markdown is command-specific — delegate to trait method
            // For generic case, fall back to TOON
            let json_str = serde_json::to_string(envelope)?;
            toon::json_to_toon(&json_str).map_err(|e| CliError::Format(e.to_string()))
        }
    }
}
```

## Markdown Strategy
Most commands use TOON/JSON. Only a few (thread, message, search) benefit from markdown.
For those, the command handler should implement a `to_markdown()` method on its data type,
and format_output should check for that trait before falling back.

Consider a trait:
```rust
pub trait MarkdownRenderable {
    fn to_markdown(&self, meta: &RobotMeta, alerts: &[RobotAlert], actions: &[String]) -> String;
}
```

## Key Decisions
- TOON via `toon::json_to_toon()` — serialize to JSON first, then convert
- This is slightly less efficient than direct TOON encoding but ensures correctness
- JSON pretty-printed for human readability when explicitly requested
- Error type: extend existing CliError with a Format variant if needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- format_output produces valid JSON when format=Json
- format_output produces valid TOON when format=Toon
- format_output handles markdown-capable types via trait
- Unit tests for all 3 format paths

## What to build
Seed a benchmark database with realistic data (project, agents, messages) using
direct DB calls instead of spawning 62 CLI subprocesses.

## Current bash behavior (bench_cli.sh lines 144-177)
```bash
# 62 subprocess spawns:
am agents register --project /tmp/bench --name BlueLake ...
am agents register --project /tmp/bench --name RedFox ...
for i in $(seq 1 49); do
    am mail send --project /tmp/bench --from BlueLake --to RedFox ...
done
for i in $(seq 1 10); do
    am mail send --project /tmp/bench --from RedFox --to BlueLake ...
done
```
This takes 3-5 seconds. Direct DB calls should take <50ms.

## Rust implementation
```rust
fn seed_bench_db(db_path: &Path) -> Result<(), Error> {
    let conn = open_db_sync(db_path)?;
    ensure_schema(&conn)?;

    // Create project
    let project_id = insert_project(&conn, "/tmp/bench")?;

    // Register agents
    let blue_lake_id = insert_agent(&conn, project_id, "BlueLake", "bench", "bench")?;
    let red_fox_id = insert_agent(&conn, project_id, "RedFox", "bench", "bench")?;

    // Send 50 messages BlueLake → RedFox
    for i in 0..50 {
        insert_message(&conn, project_id, blue_lake_id, red_fox_id,
            &format!("bench message {i}"), &format!("body of message {i}"))?;
    }

    // Send 10 replies RedFox → BlueLake
    for i in 0..10 {
        insert_message(&conn, project_id, red_fox_id, blue_lake_id,
            &format!("reply {i}"), &format!("reply body {i}"))?;
    }
    Ok(())
}
```

## Implementation notes
- Use the existing open_db_sync() pattern from the CLI crate
- Import schema creation from mcp-agent-mail-db
- Use transactions for bulk inserts (single commit = fastest)
- The seeded data should be identical to what bench_cli.sh produces
  (same project path, agent names, message subjects/bodies)
- Create a temp directory for the bench DB (cleaned up after benchmarks)

## Location
crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/bench.rs (seed_bench_db function)

## Dependencies
- Needs the DB layer's insert functions (queries.rs or raw SQL)
- Uses sync SQLite (sqlmodel_sqlite), not async pool
